title: Cisco fixes SD-WAN vManage flaw exploited in zero-day attacks
blurb: Cisco patched a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw (CVE-2026-20262) exploited in zero-day attacks to escalate to root privileges.

title: Meta reportedly moves to unwind $2B Manus deal after Beijing’s demand
blurb: Meta has reportedly begun unwinding its $2B acquisition of Chinese startup Manus after Beijing ordered the deal reversed. The move underscores how Chinese regulators can block domestic AI companies from selling to US tech giants, complicating Meta's strategy of buying talent and technology to accelerate its AI efforts.

title: Apple Foundation Models
blurb: Anthropic released Claude for Foundation Models, a Swift package that exposes Claude as a server-side language model inside Apple's Foundation Models framework. Apps drive it through the same `LanguageModelSession` API used for Apple's on-device model, switching to Claude by swapping the model argument when they need larger context, frontier reasoning, or server-side tools like web search and code execution. The beta targets the iOS 27 and macOS 27 betas, with requests billed at standard Anthropic API pricing.

title: Noise infusion banned from statistical products published by Census Bureau
blurb: A proposed US bill would prohibit the Census Bureau from using noise infusion, the differential-privacy technique it adopted in 2020 to protect individual respondents from being re-identified in published statistics. The author argues the ban misunderstands the privacy-accuracy tradeoff, since older methods like swapping also distort data while offering weaker, unquantifiable guarantees against reconstruction attacks.

title: Chinese hackers hijack auth flow, spy on isolated network for a decade
blurb: Chinese hackers hijacked an organization's authentication stack and persisted on an isolated network for a decade with full visibility into admin activity.

title: A low-carbon computing platform from your retired phones
blurb: Google Research turned a cluster of retired Android phones into a working low-carbon computing platform, repurposing devices that would otherwise become e-waste into reusable compute nodes. The prototype runs distributed workloads on the old hardware, cutting both the embodied carbon of new servers and the disposal footprint of discarded phones, with the team open-sourcing the design for others to build on.

title: Iroh 1.0
blurb: **Iroh 1.0 is the first stable release of the Rust library for direct peer-to-peer connections, built on QUIC with automatic NAT hole-punching and relay fallback.** The 1.0 milestone locks the API and protocol with backward-compatibility guarantees, letting developers dial a peer by public key and get an encrypted connection whether the two machines reach each other directly or route through a relay.

title: OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack
blurb: Three Awesome Motive WordPress plugins—OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage—were compromised via a supply-chain attack on the vendor's CDN.

title: The adder at the heart of Intel's 8087 floating-point chip
blurb: A reverse-engineering analysis reconstructs the binary adder at the core of Intel's 8087, the 1980 math coprocessor that brought hardware floating-point to the x86 line. The author traces the chip's carry-lookahead circuitry from die photos, showing how Intel packed fast addition into the silicon constraints of the era.

title: A Chinese rocket breaks apart dangerously close to the Starlink constellation
blurb: A Chinese rocket broke apart in orbit near the altitude of SpaceX's Starlink constellation, likely scattering 100 to 150 new pieces of trackable debris. The fragments add to a growing collision risk in low Earth orbit, where Starlink already performs frequent automated maneuvers to dodge other objects.

title: Anthropic shuts down Fable, Mythos models following Trump admin directive
blurb: Anthropic shut down its Fable and Mythos models after a Trump administration directive citing national security concerns. The Commerce Department warned that a "jailbreak" of Fable 5 could expose capabilities deemed a threat, prompting the company to pull the models from public access.

title: Making Claude a Chemist
blurb: Anthropic shared research on adapting Claude for chemistry tasks, evaluating how well the model handles work like reaction prediction, synthesis planning, and spectral interpretation. The post details where Claude matches specialized chemistry tools and where it still falls short, and outlines the evaluations and domain-specific testing used to measure its performance on real chemical reasoning problems.

title: Meta reportedly moves to unwind $2B Manus deal after Beijing’s demand
blurb: Meta reportedly began unwinding its $2B acquisition of Manus after Beijing ordered the deal reversed. The directive reflects China's tightening control over outbound deals involving domestic AI startups, forcing Meta to dismantle a transaction it had positioned as a major expansion of its AI talent and capabilities.

title: As AI agents become employees, NewCore emerges with $66M to give them identities
blurb: NewCore launched with $66M to manage identities for AI agents, arguing agent identity will define enterprise security.

title: Pretrained to Imagine, Fine-Tuned to Act: The Rise of World-Action Models
blurb: World-action models (WAMs) extend vision-language-action robot policies by pretraining on video to predict future frames, then fine-tuning to output actions. This post argues that learning to imagine physical outcomes before learning to act gives robots a stronger prior for manipulation and planning. NVIDIA frames WAMs as a path to better generalization from limited robot demonstration data.

title: SpaceX is public: Everything you need to know post-IPO
blurb: SpaceX completed its IPO, and the S-1 finally puts numbers behind a company that has spent two decades as a private black box. The filing reveals the real split between the launch business and Starlink, who cashed in on pre-IPO secondary deals, and which early backers stand to win or lose now that the shares trade publicly. For founders, the bigger signal is that the most capital-intensive hard-tech bet of the era reached the public markets, which reopens a path that software-era venture had all but written off.

title: SpaceX’s biggest-ever IPO just grew to $85.7 billion raised
blurb: SpaceX's IPO grew to $85.7 billion raised after underwriters maxed out their share purchases, making it the largest public offering on record. The buy-up adds to an already historic total, pushing demand for the launch and Starlink business well past initial expectations. A raise of this size resets the ceiling for what a private company can pull from public markets, and signals that investor appetite for hard-tech and space infrastructure now rivals the demand once reserved for software.

title: Anthropic’s Safety Superpower
blurb: Anthropic's deep conviction in its own safety mission has become a business weapon, justifying moves that favor its commercial interests and even put it at odds with the U.S. government. The author argues that certainty about being the good actor gives a company license to act aggressively, because every decision can be framed as serving safety. The risk is that a safety identity becomes a blank check that few inside the company will question.

title: How to earn a billion dollars
blurb: The reliable way to earn a billion dollars is to start a company that grows, because wealth comes from owning a large share of something that scales, not from being paid for your time. Salary caps what you can make, while equity in a fast-growing startup compounds without a ceiling. The essay's point for founders is that the size of the outcome tracks the value you create multiplied by how much of it you keep.

title: Not everyone is using AI for everything
blurb: Most people who try AI use it occasionally, and a large share of the population isn't using it at all. Adoption has barely moved over the past year, despite the assumption that AI is now woven into everyone's daily work. The notable shift is in sentiment, with negative feelings about AI climbing as many people cite real concerns and see little perceived value in it.

title: Arch Linux Now Believes Malware Incident Under Control: More Than 1,500 Packages
blurb: Arch Linux says its AUR malware incident is now contained after more than 1,500 packages were found carrying malicious code injected through compromised maintainer accounts. The affected packages have been flagged and removed, and the team is urging users who installed anything from the AUR during the window to audit their systems for the injected payloads.

title: Apple Foundation Models
blurb: Anthropic released Claude for Foundation Models, a Swift package that conforms Claude to the `LanguageModel` protocol in Apple's Foundation Models framework, so apps drive it through the same `LanguageModelSession` API used for Apple's on-device model. Requests go straight to the Claude API rather than through Apple, and developers swap between on-device and server-side models per session to add larger context, frontier reasoning, or server tools like web search and code execution.

title: Boosting MoE Training Throughput with Advanced Fusion Kernels
blurb: NVIDIA's fused kernels cut MoE training overhead by combining grouped GEMM, activation, and routing operations into single GPU passes.

title: North Korean Hackers Are Turning Developer Tools Into Malware Delivery Channels
blurb: Proofpoint flagged two phishing campaigns from the North Korean threat cluster Contagious Interview, which lures developers with fake recruiter outreach and code review requests before delivering malware through trusted dev tooling. The activity overlaps with the group also tracked as Famous Chollima and Void Dokkaebi, signaling that job-application and code-review workflows are now active attack surfaces engineers should treat with the same suspicion as unsolicited email attachments.

title: Java's Project Valhalla finally lands a preview in JDK 28
blurb: Java's Project Valhalla lands its first preview in JDK 28, introducing value classes that drop object identity to let the JVM flatten data inline and cut memory overhead. Architect Brian Goetz cautioned that the feature will likely remain in preview through the next LTS release, so production use is still some way off.

title: Formal methods and the future of programming
blurb: Jane Street details how it applies formal methods across its codebase, using techniques like type-driven design, property-based testing, and verification tools to catch bugs that conventional testing misses. The series argues that lightweight formal methods are now practical for everyday production code rather than confined to academia or safety-critical systems, with OCaml's type system doing much of the heavy lifting.

title: Every Frame Perfect
blurb: The author argues that smooth, jank-free interfaces are an achievable engineering standard rather than a luxury, and that most modern apps drop frames not because hardware is slow but because developers stop measuring once something looks "good enough." He makes the case for treating the frame budget as a hard constraint, profiling animations and scrolling against it, and rejecting the assumption that faster machines excuse sloppy rendering.

title: Openrouter Fusion API
blurb: OpenRouter released Fusion, a single API endpoint that dynamically routes each request across multiple frontier models rather than pinning calls to one provider. The model is exposed under the standard OpenRouter chat completions interface, so existing integrations can swap to it without code changes while OpenRouter handles model selection and failover behind the slug.

title: What’s !important #13: @function, alpha(), CSS Wordle, and More
blurb: Roundup of recent CSS features including @function, the alpha() color function, Grid Lanes, lesser-known dialog behaviors, and a CSS-built Wordle.

title: Iroh 1.0
blurb: Iroh 1.0 is a Rust library for building direct peer-to-peer connections, using QUIC with NAT hole-punching and falling back to relay servers when a direct path can't be established. The 1.0 release marks a stable API and on-wire protocol, with the relay fallback meaning connections still succeed even when both peers sit behind restrictive NATs.

title: Curl will not accept vulnerability reports during July 2026
blurb: Curl maintainer Daniel Stenberg announced the project will reject all vulnerability reports during July 2026, giving its small team a month of uninterrupted recovery from a steady stream of low-quality, often AI-generated submissions. Reporters who find a genuine flaw must hold it until August, a trade-off Stenberg frames as necessary to prevent burnout across the volunteer-run security process.

title: Running DOS on Behringers DDX3216 with a DIY x86-Bios from Scratch
blurb: The author replaced the proprietary firmware on Behringer's DDX3216 digital mixer by writing an x86 BIOS from scratch, enabling the board's embedded processor to boot DOS. The post covers reverse-engineering the hardware, implementing the BIOS interrupt routines and memory initialization needed to bring up the chipset, and the debugging required to get a stock DOS to run on the repurposed console.

title: Improving Arm64 support in CNCF projects with OCI credits
blurb: The CNCF is funding Arm64 build and test support across its hosted projects using donated Oracle Cloud Infrastructure credits, addressing a gap as Arm instances surpass 50% of new AWS launches and 33% on Azure. The credits give maintainers access to native Arm64 CI runners, removing the cost and emulation overhead that has slowed multi-architecture support in cloud-native tooling.

title: Typst 0.15.0
blurb: Typst released version 0.15.0 of its markup-based typesetting system, a faster, scriptable alternative to LaTeX that compiles documents incrementally and ships a built-in scripting language for layout, math, and figures. The release continues to target academic and technical writing with markdown-like source syntax, sub-second recompiles on edits, and a package ecosystem that replaces the sprawl of LaTeX distributions.

title: AI coding at home without going broke
blurb: The author walks through building a local AI coding setup that avoids the recurring cost of frontier API subscriptions, covering hardware choices, open-weight model selection, and the tooling needed to wire them into an editor workflow. The post weighs the trade-offs between local inference and hosted models, noting where self-hosting holds up for everyday coding tasks and where it still falls short of paid frontier offerings.

title: I indexed 669 GB of my GoPro videos using my M1 Max computer and local ML models
blurb: A developer indexed 669 GB of personal GoPro footage entirely on an M1 Max using local ML models, building a searchable archive without sending video to any cloud service. The pipeline extracts frames, runs them through on-device vision models to generate embeddings and captions, and stores the results so clips can be retrieved by natural-language queries.

title: Even more batteries included with Emacs
blurb: Emacs continues absorbing external tools into built-in features, covering completion, project management, and tree-sitter support without third-party packages.

title: Hetzner Price Adjustment
blurb: Hetzner published a price adjustment for its cloud servers, raising monthly rates across its instance lineup and citing rising energy, hardware, and infrastructure costs. The change drew sharp discussion among developers who run side projects and production workloads on Hetzner specifically to avoid hyperscaler pricing, with many weighing whether the gap to AWS, GCP, and Azure still justifies staying.

title: Flatpak-NG sounds like bad news for systemd refuseniks
blurb: The next iteration of Flatpak, dubbed Flatpak-NG, is being reworked around systemd components, which could break compatibility for distributions that use alternative init systems. The change would push systemd-free distros like Devuan, Artix, and Void Linux further from the mainstream Linux app-packaging ecosystem, narrowing the options for users who deliberately avoid systemd.

title: Noise infusion banned from statistical products published by Census Bureau
blurb: Federal legislation now bars the US Census Bureau from applying noise infusion, the differential privacy technique it adopted for the 2020 Census to protect respondent confidentiality by injecting calibrated statistical noise into published tables. The ban reverses a controversial methodology shift that data users had criticized for distorting small-area counts, but it reopens the disclosure-avoidance question of how the bureau will prevent reconstruction attacks that recover individual records from aggregate statistics.

title: Accelerating researchers and developers building multilingual AI with a new open dataset
blurb: GitHub published a repository-level dataset under CC0-1.0 that maps multilingual developer content across READMEs, issues, and pull requests, giving teams a discovery layer for non-English code documentation. The release targets researchers and developers training multilingual AI, surfacing where natural-language signals live inside repositories rather than treating code corpora as English-only.

title: The experience of rendering Arabic typography and its technical debt
blurb: Arabic typography requires cursive joining, contextual letterforms, and full bidirectional layout, demands that most rendering stacks bolt on after the fact rather than design around. The author walks through where that technical debt surfaces, from broken letter-shaping in common UI frameworks to the gap between Unicode's logical order and the visual order text must actually render in.

title: Typst 0.15.0
blurb: Typst released version 0.15.0 of its markup-based typesetting system, the open-source alternative to LaTeX that compiles plain-text source into print-ready PDFs with a scripting model designers can actually read. The release continues work on HTML export and PDF accessibility tagging, extending Typst beyond academic papers toward the styled reports, specs, and documentation that design teams produce alongside their interface work.

title: Fluent AI, Liquid Glass, flaw as a feature, AX Design
blurb: A weekly designer reading list led by an essay arguing AI-generated design fails not by being ugly but by being fluent.

title: LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers
blurb: Researchers at Obsidian Security disclosed a chain of three vulnerabilities in LiteLLM, the open-source AI gateway brokering calls to over 100 model providers, that lets a default low-privilege account escalate to full admin and achieve remote code execution on the proxy server. A server takeover exposes every stored provider API key, so operators should patch immediately, audit account privileges, and rotate all credentials held by the gateway.

title: Users cry foul after AMD stripped memory crypto from its consumer CPUs
blurb: AMD silently removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from its consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving DRAM contents unencrypted and exposing systems to cold-boot and physical memory-extraction attacks that the feature was meant to mitigate. The change was undocumented and appears deliberate, so defenders relying on at-rest memory encryption for laptops or kiosks should verify TSME support per SKU rather than assume parity with prior generations.

title: OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack
blurb: Attackers compromised Awesome Motive's CDN by exploiting a known UpdraftPlus flaw on a marketing server holding CDN credentials, then used the stolen API key to inject malicious JavaScript into OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage plugin files served to over 1.2 million WordPress sites. The script ran when admins browsed infected pages, stealing auth tokens to create rogue admin accounts and install a self-hiding backdoor with a web shell and PHP execution.

title: Google Chrome is closing the loopholes that let old ad blockers keep working
blurb: Chrome 150 and 151, arriving in late June and July, will remove the last workarounds for running Manifest V2 ad blockers like uBlock Origin.

title: XP-era Windows spotted haunting London's driverless railway
blurb: Windows XP-era systems are still running on London's driverless Docklands Light Railway, exposing aging unsupported software in critical transit infrastructure.

title: Implementing workload identity with HashiCorp Vault and SPIFFE
blurb: HashiCorp published a guide to using Vault as a SPIFFE identity issuer and broker for workload identity and authorization. Vault issues SPIFFE-format SVIDs and brokers trust between workloads, while SPIRE handles node and workload attestation at the edge. The walkthrough clarifies where each component fits, letting teams replace long-lived secrets and static credentials with short-lived, attested identities across heterogeneous environments.

title: Cloud CISO Perspectives: The 4 lessons that guided AI Threat Defense
blurb: Google Cloud's new CISO Chris Betz outlined four lessons from building AI Threat Defense, the company's system for applying AI to defensive operations. The takeaways center on using AI to accelerate analyst triage and detection at scale, while keeping human judgment in the loop for high-stakes decisions. Betz frames the effort as pushing AI toward concrete defender advantages rather than treating it solely as an attacker capability.

title: FBI disrupts massive AI-powered phishing service using a million URLs
blurb: The FBI, in coordination with Google and Black Lotus Labs, dismantled a Chinese phishing-as-a-service operation called Outsider Enterprise that ran thousands of sites across roughly a million URLs to steal credit card data and passwords. The takedown removes infrastructure that lowered the technical bar for credential theft, raising the baseline of AI-assisted phishing that enterprise security teams now have to defend against.

title: Google says a Chinese-linked hacking group targeted US and Canadian academic, medical, and military research institutions from September 2023 to November 2025 (A.J. Vicens/Reuters)
blurb: Google attributed a two-year cyber-espionage campaign to a Chinese-linked hacking group that stole data from US and Canadian academic, medical, and military research institutions between September 2023 and November 2025. The disclosure raises the stakes for IT and security leaders at research-heavy organizations, who face renewed pressure to harden access controls and audit long-lived intrusions across sensitive research environments.

title: NewCore, which helps companies manage both human and AI agent identities in a single system, emerges from stealth with a $66M seed led by Cyberstarts (Jagmeet Singh/TechCrunch)
blurb: NewCore emerged from stealth with a $66M seed led by Cyberstarts, offering a single system to manage both human and AI-agent identities. The funding targets the agent-auth gap where existing IAM stacks from Okta, Microsoft Entra, and Ping treat machine and human identities separately, a problem enterprises face as they deploy autonomous agents across production systems.

title: Most CISOs Report Pressure to Bury Bad Security News
blurb: Most CISOs report being pressured by executive leadership to downplay or delay disclosure of bad security news, according to new survey data from Dark Reading. The finding points to a governance gap that puts regulated enterprises at odds with SEC breach-disclosure rules and erodes board-level visibility into actual risk. For IT leaders, the takeaway is that incident-reporting incentives, not just tooling, determine whether security problems surface in time to act on them.

title: Midsize companies expect AI gains, but integration woes curb progress
blurb: Midsize firms expect AI productivity gains, but security, data readiness, and integration gaps continue to slow broader adoption.

title: Introducing the OpenAI Partner Network
blurb: OpenAI launched the Partner Network and committed $150M to help consultancies and systems integrators deploy enterprise AI for their clients. The program gives partners co-selling support, technical resources, and go-to-market funding, positioning OpenAI against the established Microsoft, Accenture, and Deloitte channel relationships that enterprises already lean on for AI rollouts.

title: XP-era Windows spotted haunting London's driverless railway
blurb: Windows XP-era systems are still running London's Docklands Light Railway, the city's driverless transit network.

title: Cloud CISO Perspectives: The 4 lessons that guided AI Threat Defense
blurb: Google Cloud named Chris Betz its new CISO, and his first Cloud CISO Perspectives lays out four lessons drawn from building AI Threat Defense around using AI to the defender's advantage. The piece argues that AI's security value comes from operational discipline rather than model capability alone, framing how Google positions its security stack for enterprise buyers weighing AI-driven threat detection.

title: LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers
blurb: Researchers at Obsidian Security disclosed a chain of three vulnerabilities in LiteLLM that lets a default low-privilege account escalate to full admin and execute code on the proxy server. Because LiteLLM brokers calls to more than 100 model providers behind one OpenAI-compatible interface, a takeover exposes every provider API key it holds, making the gateway a single point of failure worth patching immediately.

title: Implementing workload identity with HashiCorp Vault and SPIFFE
blurb: HashiCorp published a guide to using Vault as a SPIFFE identity issuer and broker, issuing SVIDs to workloads so services authenticate by verified identity rather than long-lived secrets. The post draws a clear boundary for where SPIRE belongs, with Vault handling issuance and authorization brokering while SPIRE manages node and workload attestation. The payoff is fewer static credentials to rotate and a consistent identity layer across clusters.

title: 21 years and counting of 'eight fallacies of distributed computing' (2025)
blurb: The author revisits Peter Deutsch's eight fallacies of distributed computing and argues they remain accurate 21 years on, walking through how each false assumption (the network is reliable, latency is zero, bandwidth is infinite) still surfaces in modern outages. The piece maps the fallacies onto current cloud and microservice architectures, making the case that no amount of abstraction has retired the underlying physics that SREs debug in production.

title: Hetzner Price Adjustment
blurb: Hetzner raised prices across its cloud servers, dedicated hardware, and storage products, citing higher energy, hardware, and infrastructure costs as the drivers behind the adjustment. The increase narrows the cost gap that has made Hetzner a default budget alternative to AWS and GCP for teams running their own k8s and CI fleets, and the 574-comment HN thread reflects how sensitive that pricing has been to infra-team build-versus-rent math.

title: OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack
blurb: Three Awesome Motive WordPress plugins, OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage, were compromised in a supply-chain attack on the company's CDN, exposing sites that rely on them for email capture, social proof, and push notifications. Marketing teams running these tools should audit installs, rotate API keys, and review CDN-loaded scripts, since the affected plugins sit directly in the lead-capture and conversion stack.

title: Electronic Arts launches EA Advertising, letting brands integrate directly into gameplay through dynamic, real-time placements, including stadium signage (Assiatou Hann/CNBC)
blurb: Electronic Arts launched EA Advertising, an in-game ad platform that places brands into gameplay through dynamic, real-time formats like stadium signage across its sports and entertainment titles. The move opens EA's player base to advertisers seeking contextual placements inside live game environments, putting it in direct competition with Roblox, Activision, and other publishers building ad businesses around their audiences.

title: How difficult could it be to design a chatbot?
blurb: A study on blood-donation chatbots found culture-tailored framing can backfire: leading with personal benefit undermined the altruistic motivation to donate.

title: Meta’s new ‘AI Mode’ on Facebook pulls from public info across its platforms
blurb: Meta began rolling out a suite of AI features on Facebook, including an "AI Mode" that draws on public information across its platforms to answer questions and surface content. The move is Meta's bid to close the gap with rivals in the AI race while keeping users engaged longer, signaling that assistant-style discovery is becoming table stakes inside consumer social products.

title: UK unveils sweeping social media ban for users under 16
blurb: The UK proposed a ban on social media use for anyone under 16, covering Snapchat, TikTok, YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, and X. Product teams at those platforms now face age-verification requirements that could reshape onboarding flows, account-recovery design, and how growth metrics are reported for any product with a meaningful UK teen user base.

title: Not everyone is using AI for everything
blurb: Gabriel Weinberg argues that AI adoption is following the same uneven curve as past technologies, with a small set of power users running most workflows through it while the majority reach for it only occasionally or not at all. The takeaway for PMs is to stop designing as if every user wants AI woven into every task, and instead segment by actual usage intensity and treat heavy AI use as one persona rather than the default.

title: Show HN: Paca – Lightweight Jira alternative for human-AI collaboration
blurb: Paca is an open-source, lightweight Jira alternative built around human-AI collaboration, structuring work so that both people and AI agents can pick up, track, and update tasks in the same system. The project hit 168 points on Hacker News, positioning itself for teams that want issue tracking designed for agent workflows rather than retrofitted onto a tool built for humans alone.

title: FBI: Fraudsters use couriers to steal money in crypto scams
blurb: The FBI warned that criminals running cryptocurrency investment scams, also known as pig butchering or romance baiting, are now dispatching in-person couriers to collect cash and assets directly from victims. The shift to physical pickups follows tighter exchange and bank controls on wire transfers, giving fraud rings a way to move funds while sidestepping electronic transaction monitoring.

title: Kraken launches crypto perps trading in the US on Kraken Pro
blurb: Kraken launched perpetual futures trading for US customers on Kraken Pro, offering crypto perps through the CFTC-regulated infrastructure it gained by acquiring Bitnomial, a licensed exchange, clearinghouse, and brokerage, in May. The move gives Kraken a domestic, fully regulated venue for leveraged derivatives, a product US traders previously accessed mainly through offshore platforms.

title: Venice and Morpheus tokens climb as US ban on Anthropic’s Fable 5 fuels ‘permissionless AI’ pitch
blurb: Venice and Morpheus tokens rose after the US ban on Anthropic's Fable 5, which both projects pitched as a case for permissionless AI.

title: Kraken launches crypto perps trading in the US on Kraken Pro
blurb: Kraken launched perpetual futures trading for US customers on Kraken Pro, enabled by its May acquisition of Bitnomial, a CFTC-licensed exchange, clearinghouse, and brokerage. The launch gives Kraken a regulated path to offer crypto perps domestically, a product US traders previously accessed mostly through offshore venues operating outside CFTC oversight.

title: Canadian payments company Nuvei agrees to acquire NY-based cross-border payments company Payoneer for ~$2.75B in cash; the deal is expected to close in mid-2027 (Reuters)
blurb: Nuvei agreed to acquire cross-border payments firm Payoneer for ~$2.75B in cash, with the deal expected to close in mid-2027. The acquisition pairs Nuvei's merchant-acquiring and global payout infrastructure with Payoneer's cross-border account network serving SMBs and marketplace sellers, consolidating two players competing for international settlement flows.

title: Bitcoin tops $65,000 as reports of US-Iran peace deal ease geopolitical fears
blurb: Bitcoin topped $65,000 as reports of a US-Iran peace deal eased geopolitical fears and lifted risk sentiment.

title: Bernstein says Robinhood could see ‘strong tailwinds’ as World Cup drives record prediction market volumes
blurb: Bernstein projects Robinhood's prediction market revenue will climb from $150M in 2025 to $586M in 2026, citing record event-contract volumes driven by the World Cup. The analysts see strong tailwinds for the category as Robinhood expands beyond equities and crypto into prediction markets, a segment now contributing materially to its trading revenue mix.
